artifacts.content:
Elena,

Just got back from PT and I’m icing on the couch, so figured I’d actually write you a proper update instead of mumbling half-asleep when you get home from school.

Knee-wise, they’re still following the post‑op protocol for the left ACL reconstruction with hamstring autograft and meniscal debridement Dr. Pierce did on 11/10/25 at Baylor Scott & White Frisco. Today was more gait training and some blood flow restriction therapy on the quad. He reminded me again: no pivoting/twisting on the left leg for the first 12 weeks, no running or jumping yet, and definitely no open‑chain knee extensions 0–30° with resistance until we’re past 8 weeks. Basically, still the “don’t be a hero” phase.

We talked a lot about work, actually. I told him how I sit 8–9 hours a day at Vertex, mostly on that hybrid schedule (three days in the Uptown office, two at home). He wants me standing or walking every 45–60 minutes, even during planning meetings. I set a few more reminders on my phone to get up between Jira tickets. He also liked the idea of the little notebook I’ve been keeping—morning heel slides and quad sets, then whatever I manage at lunch and after dinner.

I also mentioned the goals on our fridge: walking stairs without a limp by March, surviving a full 2‑hour movie without my knee turning to concrete, then maybe starting the return‑to‑running program around February if strength and balance look good. He said the soccer stuff—cutting, lateral shuffles, deceleration—won’t even be on the table until way later, probably closer to fall 2026, but he sounded optimistic as long as I don’t overdo it.

Tonight I’ll just do a light home circuit and then maybe we can watch something short so I’m not stuck in one position forever.

Love you,
Michael

Apple Notes – Health / Rehab master list

Diagnosis / surgery
- Status post left ACL reconstruction with hamstring autograft + meniscal debridement and chondral surface eval by Dr. Jonathan K. Pierce on 11/10/25.
- Main issue now: left knee pain + weakness, stiffness after long Vertex days (8–9 hrs at computer).

PT plan (from script + what Emily Thompson, MD, circled):
- Frequency: 3x/week for ~10 weeks.
- Evaluate/treat, therapeutic exercise, passive/active/assisted ROM, progressive resistive exercise, strengthening, stabilization program.
- Posture/body mechanics, gait training, fall risk assessment, home exercise program.
- Manual therapy: soft tissue and joint mobilization (no myofascial mobilization checked).
- Neuromuscular re‑ed + balance/proprioceptive training.
- Modalities: electrical stimulation and blood flow restriction therapy allowed; no ultrasound, phonophoresis, iontophoresis, or mechanical traction.
- Sports‑specific training later (soccer cuts, lateral shuffles, controlled decel) + structured return‑to‑running program around 12 weeks post‑op IF strength + control are there.

Precautions I need to remember:
- No pivoting/twisting on left leg for first 12 weeks.
- No running/jumping until surgeon clears.
- Avoid resisted open‑chain knee extension 0–30° for first 8 weeks.
- Watch for increased swelling, redness, warmth in knee/calf; stop if sharp joint pain, locking, or giving way.

Other medical stuff (for my own memory):
- Mild exercise‑induced asthma – albuterol inhaler in gym bag; rarely need it now.
- Meds: ibuprofen 400–600 mg PRN (try to keep ≤1x/day, 3–4 days/week); occasional acetaminophen 500 mg, don’t stack doses.
- No diabetes, heart disease, lung disease, DVT history.

Goals (me + Elena):
- Short term (2–3 mo): walk without limp, normal stairs, sit through 2‑hr meeting, safe bike/light leg press/bodyweight squats.
- Long term (6–9 mo): rec soccer 1–2x/week + light hikes with Elena without fear of knee giving out.

Mamí,

Solo quería ponerte al día mientras tengo un rato tranquilo. La rodilla va mejor poco a poco. El cirujano, el Dr. Pierce, hizo la reconstrucción del ligamento cruzado anterior izquierdo con injerto de isquiotibiales y le limpiaron un poco el menisco el 10 de noviembre. Ahora estoy en terapia física tres veces por semana por unas diez semanas.

La doctora que firma el plan, Emily R. Thompson, MD, marcó casi todo: ejercicios terapéuticos, rango de movimiento pasivo y activo, fortalecimiento progresivo, entrenamiento de la marcha, equilibrio, etc. Nada de correr ni saltar todavía y tengo que evitar movimientos de giro con la pierna izquierda por lo menos los primeros tres meses. También me dijeron que pare de inmediato si siento dolor agudo, bloqueo o si la rodilla se “va”.

En el trabajo sigo con el horario híbrido en Vertex Analytics: tres días en la oficina cerca de Uptown y dos desde la casa aquí en Frisco. Paso 8–9 horas frente a la compu, así que la fisioterapeuta insiste en que me levante cada 45–60 minutos, aunque esté en juntas. Puse alarmas en el teléfono para acordarme.

Sigo con los ejercicios en la casa en bloques cortos: 10–15 minutos en la mañana antes de bañarme, algo ligero en la hora de la comida, y otra ronda en la noche. Elena me ayuda a no hacer trampa con el tablero de metas en el refri — cosas como subir escaleras sin cojear para marzo y poder ver una película de dos horas sin que la rodilla se ponga como piedra.

De salud general sigo bien: nada de diabetes, corazón, etc. Solo el asma leve de ejercicio, pero casi no uso el inhalador ahora. Para el dolor, tomo ibuprofeno 400–600 mg solo cuando de verdad lo necesito y a veces Tylenol, cuidando no pasarme.

Los extraño. A ver si pueden venir un fin de semana con tus enchiladas cuando te sientas con ganas.

Te quiero,
Michael

metadata.subject: Cómo va la rodilla y el trabajo

artifacts.content:
Event: PT – Vertex schedule + home program check-in
Date: 2026-01-24
Time: 7:30–8:30 a.m.
Location: Prosper Physical Therapy
Attendees: Michael

Notes for session:

1. Work/desk setup
- Review how to make my dual‑monitor setup at Vertex (Plano/Uptown office) more knee‑friendly.
- Ask for specific ideas to force myself to stand/walk every 45–60 minutes during 2‑hr planning meetings. Maybe use Outlook reminders or stand for part of Zoom calls.

2. Break up home exercise program
- Need a written HEP that fits into 10–15 min blocks: before work (heel slides, quad sets), lunch (bike, gentle ROM), evening (strengthening, balance/proprioception).
- Make sure it’s realistic for hybrid days vs. fully remote.

3. Soreness/swelling rules
- Get clearer guidance on what level of post‑exercise soreness + swelling is “normal” vs. when to back off or call someone, since I tend to push through discomfort.
- Ask about using ibuprofen 400–600 mg and occasional 500 mg acetaminophen safely—spacing doses, daily limits.

4. Sport/long‑term goals
- Confirm rough timing for starting a return‑to‑running program around Feb 2026 if strength/neuromuscular control look good.
- Ask when we can start very basic soccer‑specific drills (no cutting yet, maybe just ball work and light lateral shuffles) so I have something to look forward to.

5. Communication stuff
- Remind them I like detailed explanations of what each exercise does, how it protects the graft, and how it gets me back to rec soccer + light hikes with Elena.

Bring: notebook with exercise log and questions list.

Danny,

Figured I’d send a longer update instead of a bunch of random texts.

Knee: I’m officially in that middle phase of rehab after the left ACL reconstruction with hamstring autograft + meniscal cleanup Dr. Pierce did on 11/10/25. PT is 3x/week for about 10 weeks. They’ve got me doing all the usual stuff—therapeutic exercise, progressive resistive work, blood flow restriction, balance/proprioception, gait training. No running or jumping until they clear me, and I still have to avoid any twisting/pivoting on the left for the first 12 weeks. The PT keeps reminding me: if I feel sharp joint pain, locking, or the knee giving way, I’m done for the day.

Work: Vertex is…Vertex. I’m back on the hybrid schedule—three days at the Dallas office near Uptown, two from the little home office here in Frisco. Sitting 8–9 hours is killing my knee more than the exercises do. I set timers to stand every 45–60 minutes and sometimes take stand‑up meetings literally standing because otherwise I forget.

Family: Mom and Dad still call every Sunday to check on the knee. They’re coming up next month, probably with half of San Antonio’s food supply. Elena’s being her usual organized‑teacher self—she basically turned our fridge into a goal board: short‑term stuff like “climb stairs normally by March” and long‑term like “back to rec soccer by early fall 2026.”

Health in general is boring, which is good: no diabetes, no heart issues, no lung disease beyond the mild exercise‑induced asthma. I keep the albuterol inhaler in my gym bag out of habit, but since I’m not doing high‑intensity cardio yet, I almost never use it. Pain‑wise I’m trying to stick to ibuprofen 400–600 mg only on the really achy days (maybe 3–4 days a week max) and the occasional 500 mg Tylenol if it spikes.

How’s Austin treating you? Send pics of your new office setup—I need ideas for making mine less “sit here until your knee turns to concrete.”

– M

Pocket notebook – Daily log 2026-01-19

Morning
6:30 a.m. – Woke up (knee stiff but less than last week).
6:40–6:55 – Home HEP block #1:
- Heel slides x20
- Quad sets x20 (really focusing on full contraction)
- Gentle active‑assisted knee flexion with strap

Workday (remote)
7:30 – Coffee + saw Elena off to Frisco ISD.
8:00–12:00 – Vertex work (data viz bug fixes). Set 45‑min timers to stand:
- 8:45: stood during Zoom stand‑up.
- 9:30: walked to kitchen, refilled water.
- 10:20: 5 min walk around apartment.
- 11:15: stairs in building (one flight up/down holding rail, focus on gait).

12:15–12:30 – HEP block #2:
- Stationary bike 10 min, easy resistance.
- Mini‑squats (bodyweight only) x2 sets of 10.
- Weight shifts side‑to‑side (balance focus).

Afternoon
1:00–5:00 – Deep focus on new dashboard feature. Forgot to stand once and knee felt “full” by 4:30. Need better reminder.

Evening
6:15–6:30 – HEP block #3:
- Straight‑leg raises x3 sets of 10.
- Bridges x3 sets of 10.
- Single‑leg stance (left) near counter, 3 x 20 sec.

Pain/swelling
- Took ibuprofen 400 mg at 7:00 p.m. (first dose today).
- Mild swelling by night but no sharp pain, locking, or giving way.

Notes
- Sitting through 90‑min planning meeting was borderline; 2‑hr block would have been too much. Goal: tolerate full 2‑hr meeting by March.
- Remember: no running/jumping yet, no resisted open‑chain knee extension 0–30°. Ask PT Wednesday if I can bump bike intervals slightly.
- Long‑term: keep eyes on hiking with Elena by late summer and rec soccer return early fall 2026.

Sof,

Catching up while I ice the knee and watch terrible YouTube highlights.

The PT crew has this whole structured plan laid out for me. Right now it’s all the basics: therapeutic exercise, passive/active ROM, strengthening, stabilization, balance, gait training, a home exercise program broken into 10–15 minute chunks so I can squeeze them in before work, at lunch, and at night. Later they’ll layer in neuromuscular re‑ed and more intense balance/proprioceptive drills, then eventually a return‑to‑running program around 12 weeks post‑op if I hit their strength and control benchmarks.

They keep hammering on posture and body mechanics at my desk since I’m still doing 8–9 hour days as a senior software engineer at Vertex. On office days I drive down the Dallas North Tollway to the Uptown/Dallas office and try to take the stairs, but I’m still holding the rail and going slow. By late afternoon the knee feels tight and full if I’ve had back‑to‑back meetings and forgot to stand.

At home, Elena’s basically my accountability coach. We share that little goal board on the fridge: short‑term stuff like “walk stairs without limp by March” and “sit through a full 2‑hour movie without major stiffness,” and longer‑term like “first light hike together by late summer” and “return to rec soccer by early fall 2026.” She doesn’t let me skip the evening exercises even when my brain is fried from work.

Health‑wise everything else is pretty clean: no diabetes, heart disease, or lung disease, no history of DVT. Just the mild exercise‑induced asthma and the usual seasonal allergies (pollen/dust). I use the albuterol inhaler only if I push cardio hard in cold weather, which obviously isn’t happening yet. For pain I stick with ibuprofen 400–600 mg when it really aches and occasionally 500 mg Tylenol, making sure not to stack them wrong.

How’s OT school going? I’m collecting questions to grill you with about proprioception next time you’re in town.

Love,
Michael

From: michael.delgado@gmail.com
To: elena.delgado@gmail.com
Subject: Trying to get everything straight for the new PT clinic
Date: 2024-11-10

Elena,

I’m filling out that insanely detailed intake packet for the new PT place, and I’m trying to make sure I don’t leave out anything important (or overshare the stuff I don’t want on the front page of my chart).

For the mental health piece: Dr. Priya Menon at Baylor Scott & White Behavioral Health – Plano officially diagnosed me with recurrent major depressive disorder with panic attacks and insomnia back on 2023-11-14, though the depression and 2–3 panic attacks per week started around March 2023 after the layoffs at the Vertex Analytics, Inc. office in Plano. She also warned me on 04/18/2024 at Baylor University Medical Center in Dallas to avoid PT drills that mimic panic—anything that spikes my heart rate or makes me feel like I’m falling—because that’s what set off the stairwell blackout at the Vertex Dallas office on 02/27/2024.

I’m also going to mention that I’m still tapering off clonazepam from Frisco Behavioral Health Clinic (Dr. Aaron Feld started it after the DUI on 09/09/2022), so sessions really need to be after 10:00 a.m. and nothing that requires quick reactions for at least two hours after my 8:30 a.m. dose.

On the physical side, I should flag the vasovagal thing too—I passed out for about 45 seconds during pre-op labs at Texas Health Presbyterian Hospital Plano on 06/03/2024 just from seeing the IV, so no dry needling or research stuff with visible needles.

Can you skim this later and tell me if there’s anything you think I should leave off or rephrase?

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Trying to get everything straight for the new PT clinic

artifacts.content:
From: michael.delgado@gmail.com
To: daniel.delgado@outlook.com
Subject: The real story behind the knee (and why I’m changing some things)
Date: 2024-11-18

Hey Danny,

Since you asked how I “managed” to blow my left ACL, the answer is: it’s messy and there isn’t just one story.

On paper, I’ve got a whole menu of risk factors. Dr. Laura Chen at Baylor Scott & White – Frisco told me on 2024-04-18 that my A1c was 6.9%, so she’s calling it possible early type 2 diabetes with some diabetic peripheral neuropathy in both feet. The numbness in my toes can last 10–15 minutes after activity. She thinks that might have thrown off my balance during that non-contact ACL tear at the University of Texas at Dallas rec center pickup game on 2024-04-20.

Then there’s the alcohol piece. The ER note from Texas Health Presbyterian Hospital Plano after the Vertex Analytics happy hour at The Rustic in Dallas on 2024-05-02 literally says “patient intoxicated at time of fall.” I’d had like 6–8 drinks over ~4 hours. Green Oaks Outpatient Services in Dallas ended up diagnosing me with alcohol use disorder, mild to moderate, and my early remission date is 2024-06-10 after their 8-week, twice-weekly outpatient program.

Layer on top of that the ADHD diagnosis from UT Southwestern Psychiatry Clinic (combined type, 2022-09-09, on lisdexamfetamine 40 mg daily), which my ortho at Texas Orthopaedic Associates (Plano office) thinks feeds my impulsive “sure, let’s play 2 hours of full-court without a brace” decisions.

So yeah, not just a freak play. I’m trying to actually respect all of this now.

– M

metadata.subject: The real story behind the knee (and why I’m changing some things)

artifacts.content:
Apple Notes – “What new PT needs to know (don’t forget)”
Last edited: 2024-11-22

1) Mental health / context
- Major depressive disorder, recurrent, moderate, with panic attacks + insomnia. Formally diagnosed by Dr. Priya Menon at Baylor Scott & White Behavioral Health – Plano on 2023-11-14, but symptoms started around March 2023.
- Blacked out at Frisco Athletic Center, Frisco, TX, on 2024-05-02 during a late-night solo basketball session after a workplace panic attack at the Vertex Analytics, Inc. office in Plano, TX. That blackout + fall is one version of how I tore the left ACL.
- Separate but related: company layoffs at Vertex in March 2023 → ongoing panic. Menon (now seen at Baylor University Medical Center in Dallas) said 04/18/2024 to avoid PT that triggers hyperventilation / racing heart.

2) Marriage / behavior stuff
- Couples counseling at LifePath Systems in McKinney since 2023-10-03 with Dr. Hannah Ruiz (weekly 60-minute sessions).
- Her 2024-05-15 note: working diagnosis = adjustment disorder with mixed anxiety and depressed mood related to marital conflict.
- She links the 2024-05-02 ACL tear at Frisco Athletic Center to “risk-seeking behavior following a heated argument” with Elena at home (1483 Willow Bend Drive) earlier that evening.

3) Other precautions
- Vasovagal syncope with needles: passed out ~45 seconds during pre-op labs at Texas Health Presbyterian Hospital Plano on 06/03/2024 after seeing IV in left arm. Tell PT: no dry needling, no research blood draws; if anything needle-related is unavoidable, I must be lying down.
- Prior low-back injury from 01/19/2021 rear-end collision on Dallas North Tollway near Warren Pkwy (ongoing State Farm claim). Ask them to avoid heavy lumbar traction or aggressive loading while focusing on ACL.
- Caregiver: I drive Mom (Rosa Martinez Delgado) to radiation at Texas Oncology–Frisco starting 11/04/2024, M/W/F 3:00–4:30 p.m. → No sedating modalities or meds beforehand; must be safe to drive.

metadata.title: What new PT needs to know (don’t forget)
